answer:
**Several works in the corpus—primarily the ancient Indic *Dharmashāstra* texts known as Smṛtis—engage directly with how the dead are remembered through detailed prescriptions for ancestral rites (*pitṛkṛya* or *śrāddha*), funeral oblations (*pindadāna*), last rites, and offerings that satisfy and sustain the deceased ancestors (*pitṛs*). These rituals ensure the dead are honored, fed, and "remembered" perpetually via sons, food gifts, and periodic commemorations, preventing their descent into hellish states and securing family merit.[^1][^2][^3][^4]**

**Key examples include:**

- **Aṅgirasa Smṛti**: Extensively covers *śrāddha* rites "for the satisfaction of the ancestors," including eclipse-timed offerings, tree-planting gifts beloved by ancestors, and last rites even for unclaimed corpses, with rules for sons performing them on behalf of fathers or grandfathers.[^5][^6][^7][^8]

- **Manusmṛti**: Discusses offerings to "gods [and] ancestors" as part of daily duties, funeral pyre rites, and purification after handling the dead, framing these as essential for Vedic life from conception to death.[^9][^10]

- **Bṛhaspati Smṛti**: Specifies funeral cake (*pinda*) offerings by sons to save fathers from hell, inheritance rules tied to who performs these obsequies, and shares for kinsmen or pupils in their absence.[^11][^12]

- **Other Smṛtis** (Kātyāyana, Nārada, Paraśara, Viṣṇu, Yama, Yājñavalkya): Similar rules on funeral debts, cakes to maternal grandfathers or seed-fathers, and prohibitions during impurity, all presupposing ritual remembrance as a son's duty.[^13][^14]

**The Old English Elegies** touch indirectly on remembrance through elegiac lament for lost kin, halls, and civilizations—e.g., ruins evoking past builders ("Wondrous is this wall-stone... earth grows old"), fathers teaching sons, or wanderers recalling dead lords—but lack prescriptive "how" beyond poetic commemoration of transience.[^15]

**No other works (e.g., Cynewulf's saints' lives, Iqbal's poetry, or French political treatises) substantively address this via rites, fame, or memory practices; mentions of "dead" are narrative or metaphorical.[^16]** The corpus's 37 works emphasize Indic ritual law here over Western literary or philosophical reflection.

[^1]: Manusmṛti, ch.3, [offerings gods ancestors](works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-17c06a).
[^2]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti, ch.1, [satisfaction of the ancestors](works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-9f5b93).
[^3]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti, work wiki, [*śrāddha* श्राद्ध ang_1](works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/wiki/_work.card.md).
[^4]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, ch.1, [funeral cake rite](works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-ad27f9).
[^5]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti, ch.1, [*śrāddha*... satisfaction of the ancestors](works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-8b8d33).
[^6]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti, ch.1, [ancestors (*Pitṛs*), grandfathers](works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-b4077b).
[^7]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti, ch.1, [Last Rites for the Unclaimed Dead](works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-211dbe).
[^8]: Aṅgirasa Smṛti search hits (e.g., son offers to father's ancestors).
[^9]: Manusmṛti, ch.2, [rites... to the funeral pyre](works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-e3405d).
[^10]: Manusmṛti, ch.3 wiki, [families... attain great fame](works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/wiki/03.card.md).
[^11]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, ch.1, [funeral cake to him](works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-ad27f9).
[^12]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, ch.1, [offerer of the funeral oblation](works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-05d2bd).
[^13]: Kātyāyana Smṛti, ch.52/78, [funeral rites](works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/52/translation/#p-4f98be).
[^14]: Nārada Smṛti, ch.1, [funeral cake to... grandfather](works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-d1a156).
[^15]: Old English Elegies wiki, [earth grows old](works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/wiki/_work.card.md); ch.16, [wyrde yebraecon](works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/16/translation/).
[^16]: Corpus-wide searches ("remember the dead," "memory of the dead," "fame posterity," etc.) yielded 0 direct hits outside Smṛtis/snippets above.
